what_it_does: Acceptance test for an L5 'self-change locating' feature: it verifies that an address-filtered query (self_changes_at) joins a UI address to the code files in its S3-resolved scope and returns only self-change log entries whose changed_files intersect that scope. It also checks isolation between two non-overlapping scopes, preservation of existing self_change_log/last_self_change/revert_self_change methods, stale-corpus fail-loud behavior, revert-at-address composing the existing revert_self_change, and route exposure in runtime/bridge.py.
observations: Uses synthetic self-change records by monkeypatching suite.self_change_log to make deterministic assertions while still using the real resolve_scope corpus resolver.
Tests two real corpus addresses: ui://workshop/self-changes → runtime/suite.py and ui://canvas/portal-window → nodes/portal.py.
Includes a stale-trichotomy check by temporarily pointing suite._corpus_dir at an empty corpus directory.
Verifies bridge.py route existence by reading and grepping the source file rather than issuing HTTP requests.
Uses a global PASS counter and a helper check() that asserts and prints per-check status.
Restores real methods after each monkeypatch block to avoid cross-test contamination.
standouts: Deterministic acceptance test achieved by monkeypatching real git history while keeping the real address→code resolver.
Stale propagation is treated as a first-class fail-loud result, not a silent empty list.
Revert-at-address is explicitly scoped: it refuses a sha that did not touch the address's scope.
Source-level assertion that /api/self-changes-at exists and calls self_changes_at, while /api/revert remains the only revert gate.
conventions: Long module docstring explains the layer, design rationale, and post-carve fixture update.
Inline comments label each numbered acceptance claim from the docstring.
Uses tempfile and FsStore/NodeRegistry/Suite setup common to runtime tests.
Monkeypatch-and-restore pattern for controlling non-deterministic dependencies.
concerns: Heavy monkeypatching of production methods in an acceptance test may hide real integration behavior.
Assertions about bridge.py route behavior rely on source text grepping, which is brittle if route strings or call sites change.
The test depends on live corpus scopes remaining exactly as documented; any refactor of runtime/suite.py or nodes/portal.py could break the isolation fixture.
Setting suite._corpus_dir to a lambda returning a path is a fragile way to simulate an unreadable corpus.
Temporary directories created with tempfile.mkdtemp are not explicitly cleaned up.
notes: An acceptance test that proves a new address-scoped self-change query sits on top of existing log/revert methods without replacing them.
questions: Do the methods self_changes_at and revert_self_change_at already exist in runtime/suite.py, or is this test defining the contract they must satisfy?
How is code-symbols.json regenerated in practice, and how does the system detect staleness?
What does 'operator-only /api/revert gate' mean at the HTTP/auth layer?
Why is grepping bridge.py source preferred over an integration HTTP call for route verification?
What should happen if a self-change record has a 'matched_files' key versus only 'changed_files'?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose matches the file: it exercises the new address-filtered self-change query, isolation, preservation, stale propagation, and revert composition.
apparent_themes: Additive layering without modifying lower layers
Deterministic testing of non-deterministic git history
intention_signals: The file is trying to establish that the new L5 'what changed HERE?' capability is a thin, safe filter over existing self-change infrastructure, with deterministic synthetic fixtures and explicit handling of stale or out-of-scope cases.
novelty: The address-scoped self-change join and the stale-trichotomy rule (propagating stale/note rather than returning a silent empty list) are the distinctive ideas.
contribution: This file contributes an acceptance-test contract for self_changes_at and revert_self_change_at, including deterministic synthetic-ledger fixtures, scope-isolation assertions, stale-corpus behavior, and a source-level check that the feature is wired into runtime/bridge.py.
summary_for_embedding: An acceptance test that verifies an address-scoped self-change query joins UI addresses to S3-resolved code scopes, filters the self-change log, preserves existing log/revert methods, propagates stale corpus state, and exposes a bridge route.
intention_for_embedding: To prove the L5 self-change-locating layer is an additive, deterministic, fail-loud filter over the existing self-change ledger and revert path.

symbols.does: Acceptance-test assertion helper that increments a global pass counter, asserts a condition, and prints an 'ok' message.
symbols.description: The check function is the file's micro-assertion harness. It takes a human-readable label and a boolean condition, fails loudly with the label if the condition is false, and otherwise increments the global PASS counter and prints a success line.

symbols.does: Stand-in for suite.revert_self_change that captures the requested sha and returns a fake revert result.
symbols.description: _mock_revert is used during the revert-at-address acceptance check to prove that revert_self_change_at resolves the address-scoped sha and then delegates to the existing revert_self_change path, without performing any real git mutation.
